title: Ensure Secrets are Not Stored in Cloud Functions  Environment Variables by Using Secret Manager

input_row.title: Ensure Secrets are Not Stored in Cloud Functions  Environment Variables by Using Secret Manager
input_row.description: Google Cloud Functions allow you to host serverless code that is executed when an event is triggered, without the requiring the management a host operating system. These functions can also store environment variables to be used by the code that may contain authentication or other information that needs to remain confidential.
input_row.rationale: It is recommended to use the Secret Manager, because environment variables are stored unencrypted, and accessible for all users who have access to the code.
input_row.audit: Determine if Confidential Information is Stored in your Functions in Cleartext From Google Cloud Console 1. Within the project you wish to audit, select the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Scroll down to under the heading 'Serverless', then select 'Cloud Functions' 2. Click on a function name from the list 3. Open the Variables tab and you will see both buildEnvironmentVariables and environmentVariables 4. Review the variables whether they are secrets 5. Repeat step 3-5 until all functions are reviewed From Google Cloud CLI 1. To view a list of your cloud functions run gcloud functions list 2. For each cloud function in the list run the following command. gcloud functions describe 3. Review the settings of the buildEnvironmentVariables and environmentVariables. Determine if this is data that should not be publicly accessible. Determine if Secret Manager API is 'Enabled' for your Project From Google Cloud Console 1. Within the project you wish to audit, select the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Hover over 'APIs & Services' to under the heading 'Serverless', then select 'Enabled APIs & Services' in the menu that opens up. 2. Click the button '+ Enable APIS and Services' 3. In the Search bar, search for 'Secret Manager API' and select it. 4. If it is enabled, the blue box that normally says 'Enable' will instead say 'Manage'. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Within the project you wish to audit, run the following command. gcloud services list 2. If 'Secret Manager API' is in the list, it is enabled.
input_row.remediation: Enable Secret Manager API for your Project From Google Cloud Console 1. Within the project you wish to enable, select the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Hover over 'APIs & Services' to under the heading 'Serverless', then select 'Enabled APIs & Services' in the menu that opens up. 2. Click the button '+ Enable APIS and Services' 3. In the Search bar, search for 'Secret Manager API' and select it. 4. Click the blue box that says 'Enable'. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Within the project you wish to enable the API in, run the following command. gcloud services enable Secret Manager API Reviewing Environment Variables That Should Be Migrated to Secret Manager From Google Cloud Console 1. Log in to the Google Cloud Web Portal (https://console.cloud.google.com/) 2. Go to Cloud Functions 3. Click on a function name from the list 4. Click on Edit and review the Runtime environment for variables that should be secrets. Leave this list open for the next step. From Google Cloud CLI 1. To view a list of your cloud functions run gcloud functions list 2. For each cloud function run the following command. gcloud functions describe 3. Review the settings of the buildEnvironmentVariables and environmentVariables. Keep this information for the next step. Migrating Environment Variables to Secrets within the Secret Manager From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Secret Manager page in the Cloud Console. 2. On the Secret Manager page, click Create Secret. 3. On the Create secret page, under Name, enter the name of the Environment Variable you are replacing. This will then be the Secret Variable you will reference in your code. 4. You will also need to add a version. This is the actual value of the variable that will be referenced from the code. To add a secret version when creating the initial secret, in the Secret value field, enter the value from the Environment Variable you are replacing. 5. Leave the Regions section unchanged. 6. Click the Create secret button. 7. Repeat for all Environment Variables From Google Cloud CLI 1. Run the following command with the Environment Variable name you are replacing in the . It is most secure to point this command to a file with the Environment Variable value located in it, as if you entered it via command line it would show up in your shell’s command history. gcloud secrets create --data-file="/path/to/file.txt" Granting your Runtime's Service Account Access to Secrets From Google Cloud Console 1. Within the project containing your runtime login with account that has the 'roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor' permission. 2. Select the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Hover over 'Security' to under the then select 'Secret Manager' in the menu that opens up. 3. Click the name of a secret listed in this screen. 4. If it is not already open, click Show Info Panel in this screen to open the panel. 5.In the info panel, click Add principal. 6.In the New principals field, enter the service account your function uses for its identity. (If you need help locating or updating your runtime's service account, please see the 'docs/securing/function-identity#runtime_service_account' reference.) 5. In the Select a role dropdown, choose Secret Manager and then Secret Manager Secret Accessor. From Google Cloud CLI As of the time of writing, using Google CLI to list Runtime variables is only in beta. Because this is likely to change we are not including it here. Modifying the Code to use the Secrets in Secret Manager From Google Cloud Console This depends heavily on which language your runtime is in. For the sake of the brevity of this recommendation, please see the '/docs/creating-and-accessing-secrets#access' reference for language specific instructions. From Google Cloud CLI This depends heavily on which language your runtime is in. For the sake of the brevity of this recommendation, please see the' /docs/creating-and-accessing-secrets#access' reference for language specific instructions. Deleting the Insecure Environment Variables Be certain to do this step last. Removing variables from code actively referencing them will prevent it from completing successfully. From Google Cloud Console 1. Select the Navigation hamburger menu in the top left. Hover over 'Security' then select 'Secret Manager' in the menu that opens up. 2. Click the name of a function. Click Edit. 3. Click Runtime, build and connections settings to expand the advanced configuration options. 4. Click 'Security’. Hover over the secret you want to remove, then click 'Delete'. 5. Click Next. Click Deploy. The latest version of the runtime will now reference the secrets in Secret Manager. From Google Cloud CLI gcloud functions deploy --remove-env-vars If you need to find the env vars to remove, they are from the step where ‘gcloud functions describe ’ was run.
input_row.impact: There should be no impact on the Cloud Function. There are minor costs after 10,000 requests a month to the Secret Manager API as well for a high use of other functions. Modifying the Cloud Function to use the Secret Manager may prevent it running to completion.
input_row.default_value: By default Secret Manager is not enabled.
input_row.additional_information: There are slight additional costs to using the Secret Manager API. Review the documentation to determine your organizations' needs.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_cloud_functions_environment_variables_secrets_management

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands to verify compliance:
    1. **List all Cloud Functions**: 
       Command: `gcloud functions list`
       This will return a list of all Cloud Functions in the project.
      
    2. **Describe each function**: 
       Command: `gcloud functions describe <FUNCTION_NAME>`
       This retrieves details of the specified function including environment variables.
    
    3. **Verify environment variables**:
       In the output of the above command, specifically check the fields `buildEnvironmentVariables` and `environmentVariables`. 
       These variables must not contain any sensitive information (e.g., passwords, tokens).
    
    4. **Check if Secret Manager API is enabled**: 
       Command: `gcloud services list`
       This will list all enabled services for the project.
       Verify that `Secret Manager API` is included in the list.
  
- **Pass/Fail criteria:**
    - **Pass**: 
      - If all environment variables in all Cloud Functions do not contain any confidential information (determined by your organization's policy on what is considered "confidential").
      - If the `Secret Manager API` is enabled for the project.
    - **Fail**: 
      - If any environment variable contains classified information.
      - If the `Secret Manager API` is not enabled.

- **Example**: 
    - Run `gcloud functions describe myFunction` and ensure `environmentVariables` do not contain sensitive info like `API_KEY` or `PASSWORD`, and that `gcloud services list` shows `Secret Manager API` as enabled.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The classification as "Automated" is mostly correct, as the use of `gcloud` commands can automate the retrieval and checking of environment variables and enabled services. However, determining whether environment variables contain sensitive information might require manual policy interpretation unless specific patterns or keywords are predefined for automated checks.
- **GCP APIs/Google Cloud SDK (gcloud)/CLI:** The use of `gcloud` commands is appropriate for this task. However, the assessment lacks the use of GCP APIs directly, which could provide more flexibility and integration into broader automation frameworks.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 7

- **Automation Details:** The steps outlined are generally correct, but there are some areas for improvement:
  - The `gcloud functions describe` command will indeed show environment variables, but the method of determining if they contain sensitive information is not automated unless specific patterns are predefined.
  - The command `gcloud services list` will list enabled services, but it does not directly verify the use of Secret Manager for environment variables.
- **Program Name:** The program name `gcp_cloud_functions_environment_variables_secrets_management` is somewhat descriptive, but it could be more specific to indicate the exact nature of the check, such as `gcp_cloud_functions_env_vars_secret_check`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Automation of Sensitive Information Detection:** Consider implementing a script or tool that checks environment variables against a predefined list of sensitive patterns (e.g., regex for API keys, tokens).
- **Direct API Use:** Utilize the Google Cloud Functions API to programmatically retrieve and analyze environment variables, which could be more efficient and integrated into CI/CD pipelines.
- **Program Name Suggestion:** Rename the program to `gcp_cloud_functions_env_vars_secret_check` to better reflect the specific security check being performed.
- **Additional Checks:** Ensure that the environment variables are indeed using Secret Manager by checking for references to secrets in the environment variable values.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- The assessment covers the basic steps needed to verify the control but lacks depth in automation and specific checks for sensitive information. The reliance on manual interpretation for sensitive data detection reduces confidence in a fully automated classification.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- **Modify the Automation Details:** Enhance the automation by incorporating pattern recognition for sensitive information in environment variables and ensure that Secret Manager is being used for sensitive data.
- **Program Name Update:** Update the program name to `gcp_cloud_functions_env_vars_secret_check` for clarity.
- **Consider API Integration:** Explore using the Google Cloud Functions API for a more robust and integrated solution.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** The task of verifying whether environment variables in Google Cloud Functions contain sensitive information can be automated using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands. The `gcloud functions describe` command provides the necessary details to check environment variables, and the `gcloud services list` command can confirm if the Secret Manager API is enabled. While manual interpretation might be needed to determine if a variable is sensitive, predefined patterns or keywords can automate this process. The use of GCP APIs and CLI tools makes this feasible without manual inspection.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
gcp_cloud_functions_env_vars_secret_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: Cloud Functions
- API/CLI Method: Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands
  - `gcloud functions list`
  - `gcloud functions describe <FUNCTION_NAME>`
  - `gcloud services list`
- Properties to Check: 
  - `buildEnvironmentVariables`
  - `environmentVariables`
  - Enabled services for `Secret Manager API`
- Pass Criteria: 
  - No sensitive information in environment variables as per predefined patterns.
  - Secret Manager API is enabled.
- Fail Criteria: 
  - Presence of sensitive information in environment variables.
  - Secret Manager API is not enabled.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** The second AI opinion suggested partial automation due to the need for manual policy interpretation. However, by using predefined patterns for sensitive information, this can be automated. The first AI's approach was more aligned with GCP capabilities, leading to the decision for full automation.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Google_Cloud_Platform_Foundation_Benchmark_v4_0_0
